question: Please introduce yourself and explain what is relevant to this role.
context: Aim for a conversational 60–90 seconds, then let the interviewer choose the first area to explore.
shortAnswer: I am a senior Java backend engineer with long experience in business-critical systems and, since 2021, in the SGN3 education-management platform. My strongest areas are Java and Jakarta EE, JPA/Hibernate, PostgreSQL performance, integrations and asynchronous processing with JMS. I also work across the stack when needed and use my SinapiPRO project for hands-on Spring Boot and Angular development. What I bring to this role is experience diagnosing production behavior, modernizing legacy software incrementally and translating complex operational rules into reliable code. I am now expanding my practical depth in Kotlin, Kafka and Kubernetes, and I am interested in applying that foundation to transport logistics while learning the team's actual domain and architecture.
detailedAnswer:
A good introduction follows a clear arc: current professional identity, two or three strengths supported by evidence, one relevant project, and why those experiences connect to this role. It should not be a chronological reading of the résumé.

The wording above is intended to take roughly 60–90 seconds at a natural pace. Pause after it. If the interviewer asks for detail, choose one defensible case—such as production SQL diagnosis, JMS integration or the Spring Boot and Angular work in SinapiPRO—and explain your individual contribution.

Keep the experience boundary explicit: Jakarta EE, PostgreSQL and JMS are production experience; Spring Boot and Angular are hands-on project experience; Kotlin, Kafka and Kubernetes are areas being expanded rather than claimed production ownership.
keyPoints: Target 60–90 seconds
Lead with current engineering identity
Support strengths with relevant evidence
Distinguish production, project and study experience
End with the connection to the role
redFlags: Turns the introduction into a five-minute résumé recital
Lists technologies without showing how they were used
Claims production depth in the technologies currently being learned
Uses scale figures that cannot be defended in follow-up questions

followUps.question: Which production problem best represents your way of working?
Which part of the target stack would require the most learning at first?

question: Why are you interested in this REWE Digital position?
shortAnswer: The role combines three things that fit my direction: operationally important software, a JVM stack with Spring Boot and Kotlin, and the challenge of evolving existing applications while building new capabilities. My background in enterprise Java, SQL performance, integrations and asynchronous workflows is relevant, while logistics and the advertised Kafka and Kubernetes stack would stretch me in areas I am deliberately developing.
detailedAnswer:
I would anchor the answer in the vacancy rather than speculate about internal systems. The advertised mix of development and maintenance matters to me because much of my work has been changing business-critical software without interrupting the operation. Enterprise Java and integration work are my production foundation; Spring Boot, Kotlin, Kafka and Kubernetes are framework- or platform-specific growth areas that I should not present as equivalent experience.

I am also interested in the transport-logistics context because latency, traceability, partial failure and clear operational ownership have visible business consequences there. That is a domain motivation, not a claim that I already know TRAB's workflows. I would use the interview to validate which workflows and quality attributes the team actually owns.

One accuracy guardrail is essential: public material describing more than 170,000 REWE Markt employees is company headcount, not a count of drivers. It should not be presented as TRAB's user or system scale.
keyPoints: Connect motivation to wording in the vacancy
Explain both immediate contribution and learning edge
Treat logistics examples as domain reasoning, not inside knowledge
Show interest in evolving running systems
Do not convert company headcount into platform scale
redFlags: Generic motivation that could name any employer
Claims knowledge of TRAB internals not present in the vacancy
Says REWE has 170,000 drivers; the cited figure refers to employees
Presents the role only as a way to learn technologies

followUps.question: Which part of your current experience would help the team soonest?
What do you still need to learn about the role before deciding it is a good fit?

question: What do you understand about the TRAB context, and what remains uncertain?
shortAnswer: The vacancy confirms that TRAB is a team of ten developing transport-logistics software for distribution to more than 5,700 German supermarkets, supporting warehouse staff, drivers and logistics planners. It also says the team is developing NEO, a unified modular communication platform. I would still ask about exact workflows, service boundaries, production load, on-call ownership and what plug-and-play means technically.
detailedAnswer:
I would explicitly separate three levels.

Confirmed by the vacancy: TRAB is a ten-person product team in Transport Logistics; its solutions manage distribution to more than 5,700 supermarkets in Germany and support warehouse staff, drivers and logistics planners. The work includes web applications, APIs, backend services, integrations and analytics. The team is also developing NEO, described as a unified communication platform with a modular, plug-and-play architecture.

Reasonable domain hypotheses, not confirmed implementation facts: workflows may include dispatch, status updates, exceptions or delivery confirmation; availability and traceability are likely important; and Kafka may support some asynchronous flows. These are prompts for questions, not architecture facts.

Unknown until the team explains it: which lifecycle steps TRAB owns, current system boundaries and load, NEO's production maturity, the technical meaning of plug-and-play, on-call expectations, and how responsibilities are divided across locations and teams.
keyPoints: Label vacancy facts, domain hypotheses and unknowns separately
Keep confirmed users separate from hypothesized workflows
Use hypotheses to form questions
Ask about system ownership and operational responsibility
Avoid assuming that modular means microservices
redFlags: Describes an inferred architecture as the current architecture
Invents traffic or usage volumes beyond the vacancy
Treats transport planning and execution ownership as confirmed
Cannot name any important unknowns

followUps.question: Which uncertainty would you clarify first if you joined?
How would your approach change depending on which transport workflow the team owns?

question: Suppose a transport-status service can receive duplicate and out-of-order events. How would you make processing reliable?
shortAnswer: I would first clarify the required business ordering and source of truth. I would give each event a stable identity and aggregate key, make consumers idempotent, reject or quarantine invalid transitions, and persist processing state atomically with the business update. I would monitor duplicates, stale events, retries and reconciliation gaps rather than assuming exactly-once delivery solves the workflow.
detailedAnswer:
The first question is whether every event needs global order or only order per transport, route or stop. Usually the smaller aggregate boundary is both sufficient and more scalable. I would also ask whether events carry a source sequence, version or event time and whether late corrections are valid.

For processing, I would use a unique event identifier for deduplication and a version or sequence check on the aggregate. The state transition must be validated against domain rules: a late 'departed' event should not silently overwrite a later 'delivered' state. Depending on requirements, it may be ignored, stored for audit, used to rebuild state, or sent to a reconciliation flow.

Retries must be safe. The business update and processed-event marker should commit together where possible; an outbox or equivalent pattern can protect any subsequent publication. Dead-letter handling needs ownership and replay tooling. Metrics should expose consumer lag, retry rate, duplicate rate, invalid transitions and age of the oldest unprocessed event.

This is a general design response, not a statement about TRAB's current implementation.
keyPoints: Clarify the necessary ordering boundary
Use stable event identity and aggregate versioning
Make the business transition idempotent
Handle stale and invalid events explicitly
Design reconciliation, replay and operational metrics
redFlags: Assumes broker delivery semantics guarantee business exactly-once behavior
Orders every event globally without a requirement
Retries state changes that are not idempotent
Sends failures to a dead-letter topic with no recovery process
clarifyingQuestions: Can a late event be a valid correction?
Which system is authoritative for current status?
What is the acceptable delay before manual intervention?

followUps.question: How would you reconcile state after a consumer bug is fixed?
What changes if the database update succeeds but publishing the next event fails?

question: What can you say about NEO from the vacancy, and what would you ask before discussing its design?
shortAnswer: I can say only what the vacancy states: TRAB is developing NEO as a unified communication platform with a modular plug-and-play architecture. I do not know whether it is one deployable or many, what 'communication' means in this domain, how plugins are isolated, or its production maturity. Those are my first questions.
detailedAnswer:
The phrase 'unified communication platform' has several plausible meanings: user notifications, integration adapters, inter-service messaging or another domain-specific capability. 'Plug-and-play' could refer to in-process modules, independently deployed services, configuration-driven adapters or a combination. None of those interpretations should be presented as NEO's implementation without confirmation.

Before suggesting architecture, I would ask about producers and consumers, delivery guarantees, plugin lifecycle and compatibility, isolation between plugins, data ownership, security boundaries, observability, deployment model and current pain points. I would also ask whether NEO is already in production and which teams depend on it.

Once those answers are known, I can connect relevant patterns from my integration and messaging experience. Until then, the senior response is a precise boundary between knowledge and hypothesis.
keyPoints: Repeat only the three confirmed descriptions from the vacancy
Show that key terms have multiple possible implementations
Ask about contracts, lifecycle, isolation and guarantees
Do not assume Kafka is part of NEO
Connect experience only after clarifying the problem
redFlags: Draws the NEO architecture as though it were known
Assumes a plugin is necessarily a Spring bean or a microservice
Claims specific users, channels or Kafka topics
Uses 'plug-and-play' without asking how compatibility and failure isolation work

followUps.question: Which plugin contract concerns would you explore first?
How would the trade-offs differ between in-process and independently deployed plugins?

question: Which parts of your experience transfer to this role, and where are the limits?
shortAnswer: The strongest transfer is in reliable enterprise workflows: Java, transactions, PostgreSQL performance, REST and SOAP integrations, JMS messaging, production diagnosis and incremental legacy modernization. A measured SGN3 case reduced a request over 26,705 enrollment records in the period from about 16 minutes and 48,000 queries to about two seconds and roughly ten queries. The limit is equally clear: my production stack is mainly Jakarta EE and JMS; Spring Boot and Angular are hands-on project experience, while Kafka, Kotlin and Kubernetes are still growth areas.
detailedAnswer:
I would connect capabilities, not pretend the domains or technologies are identical. Complex education workflows and transport workflows can both demand traceability, transaction boundaries, resilience to external-system failure and careful production change. My experience diagnosing APM traces and SQL plans, designing asynchronous JMS flows and integrating external systems is directly relevant to those engineering concerns.

The performance example is evidence of method rather than claimed REWE-scale equivalence: 26,705 is the volume of enrollment records in the period, not a claim of 26,705 concurrent users or requests. The request itself generated about 48,000 queries and took about 16 minutes before the fix; pagination, query restructuring and projections reduced it to roughly ten queries and around two seconds. Every number should be used only if it can be defended from measurements.

The gaps should be named without apology. JMS concepts transfer to Kafka, but the log model, partitioning, replay and consumer-group operations require specific learning. Jakarta EE concepts transfer to Spring, but framework behavior still needs hands-on depth. That is a credible ramp-up plan, not equivalence.
keyPoints: Connect engineering capabilities rather than claiming domain equivalence
Describe the 26,705 figure as record volume, not concurrency
Use the consistent measured path: about 16 minutes to two seconds
Separate production and project experience
Name technology-specific learning gaps
redFlags: Calls 26,705 records simultaneous or concurrent without evidence
Claims JMS and Kafka are interchangeable
Presents a personal project as production operation
Uses performance figures without being able to explain their measurement

followUps.question: Which JMS lesson would you carry into Kafka, and what would you relearn?
How were the performance numbers measured and validated?

question: What questions would you ask the team at the end of the interview?
shortAnswer: I would ask questions that reveal the real work and success criteria: which transport capabilities TRAB owns, what I would likely change first, how NEO's modularity works in practice, which production failure is hardest today, how the team measures reliability, and what good performance after six months looks like.
detailedAnswer:
I would choose three or four questions based on what was not already answered. Useful options include:

• What part of the transport lifecycle does TRAB own, and where are the boundaries with other teams?
• Which application or capability would this role work on first?
• What does plug-and-play mean concretely in NEO: code modules, deployed services, adapters or something else?
• Which production failure or operational bottleneck consumes the most team attention today?
• How are architectural decisions made and recorded?
• How much of the work is modernization, feature development and production support?
• What would you expect the person in this role to have achieved after six months?

A follow-up based on something an interviewer said is usually stronger than reading a long prepared list. I would avoid questions whose answers are clearly public or already covered in the conversation.
keyPoints: Ask about ownership and boundaries
Clarify the first likely work and current pain
Use NEO questions to validate, not display assumptions
Ask how success is measured
Adapt questions to the conversation
redFlags: Asks a long list without responding to the interview discussion
Uses a question to disguise an unsupported claim
Focuses only on technologies and not users or outcomes
Asks for information already provided

followUps.question: Which one question would most affect your decision about the role?
How would you follow up if the team says its biggest issue is operational reliability?
